mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 5.0 Supercharged (508PS AJ133) engine typically costs £5,000–£12,000 supply only. Fitting adds £900–£2,500 depending on workshop location. A used 2018 SVR engine is listed at £5,995 supply only with £2,000 fitting. Main dealer new engines can exceed £12,000 supply only.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: This is one of the most controversial engines in Land Rover's modern lineup. The AJ133 5.0 V8 delivers exceptional performance but is plagued by three fatal flaws: excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles is common), timing chain tensioner failure, and supercharger seal leaks. With meticulous maintenance (oil changes every 8,000 miles) and preventative timing chain work, some examples exceed 150,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain tensioner failure (rattling on cold start, can lead to catastrophic engine failure), excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles, leading to oil starvation and spun bearings), supercharger seal leaks (oil contamination of belts, potential supercharger seizure), and water pump pulley belt failure (leading to overheating).

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 60% Land Rover scrap threshold: if your Range Rover Sport is worth £20,000 and a recon engine is £7,500 (37.5%), it's worth it. If the vehicle is a tired £12,000 example, the same engine exceeds 60% — get a scrap valuation first. Enthusiasts and collectors often justify the spend due to the desirability of the supercharged V8.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 5.0 Supercharged uses the 508PS (AJ133) — a 5.0-litre supercharged V8 producing 510–575 bhp. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the timing chain tensioners, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement (£1,200–£3,000) is far cheaper than a catastrophic failure requiring a full engine replacement (£7,000+). At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Many specialists recommend replacing the timing chain components as preventative maintenance at 80,000 miles.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the only engine generation this badge ever used — the 508PS (AJ133). See the Era Map above for era-specific pricing. Note that a used SVR engine from 2018 is listed at £5,995 supply only, with fitting at £2,000.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged engine typically takes 15–25 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The complex supercharger and V8 layout adds significant labour time compared to simpler engines.

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Failure
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking, rough running, engine warning light, misfire codes.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; catastrophic failure requires full engine replacement (£7,000+).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: at first sign of rattle, book inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The timing chain tensioners on the 5.0 AJ133 V8 are a well-documented weak point. Plastic guides degrade over time, causing chain slack that can lead to skipped timing and piston-valve contact in this interference engine. Extended oil service intervals (recommended at 10,000 miles) are "absolutely insane for a hot, big V8 engine" — they accelerate wear on the plastic guides, chain links, and tensioners. A £1,200–£3,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than a £7,000+ engine replacement.

problems.title: Excessive Oil Consumption & Oil Starvation
problems.symptoms: Low oil level warning between services, blue smoke from exhaust, knocking noise from bottom end, spun bearing failure.
problems.repairCost: £500–£2,000 (diagnosis and top-up management; oil consumption testing).
problems.replacementCost: £5,000–£12,000 (full engine replacement if bottom end fails).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil level drops rapidly.

problems.recommendation: Many AJ133 engines burn 1L of oil per 1,000 miles — Land Rover issued TSB LTB00485 (2015) acknowledging "excessive oil consumption under spirited driving conditions". If ignored, low oil leads to spun rod bearings and total bottom-end failure. Check oil levels every 500 miles and use the correct 5W-20 or 5W-30 specification.

problems.title: Supercharger Seal Leaks
problems.symptoms: Oil smell from engine bay, oil contamination on belts, supercharger whining or rattling, loss of boost.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The supercharger nose seal is a known failure point — oil leaks from the seal contaminate belts and cause slippage or failure, often misdiagnosed as "just a leak" until the supercharger seizes. A clattering or rattling noise from the supercharger can indicate torsional isolator wear. A full supercharger rebuild with upgraded seals is significantly cheaper than a complete replacement.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches fault before catastrophic failure
Regular top-ups and monitoring; bottom-end failure requires engine replacement
Full rebuild with upgraded seals restores performance
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Bottom-end failure requires full rebuild or replacement

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 5.0 Supercharged? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with oil changes every 8,000 miles or less (not the 10,000-mile interval)
* Timing chain and tensioners replaced as preventative maintenance
* Supercharger inspected and seals replaced if showing signs of wear
* No history of oil consumption warnings or bottom-end knocking
avoidIf: * Cold-start rattle or ticking — indicates timing chain tensioner wear
* Low oil level between services — indicates excessive consumption
* Whining or rattling from supercharger — seal or bearing failure
* Poor service history — these engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 5.0 Supercharged AJ133 is a glorious engine when healthy, but it's complex, thirsty, and has well-documented weak points. The timing chain tensioners, oil consumption, and supercharger seals are the three fatal flaws. A £7,500 reconditioned engine on a Range Rover Sport valued at £20,000 is 37.5% of the car's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. On a sub-£12,000 example, think carefully. The 60% Land Rover scrap threshold applies.

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Sport 5.0 Supercharged (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle or ticking on startup (timing chain tensioner wear)
* Service History — Including oil changes every 8,000 miles (not the 10,000-mile interval)
* Oil Level — Check oil level and consumption history — 1L per 1,000 miles is common
* Supercharger Noise — Any whining, rattling, or clattering from the supercharger?
* Smoke Check — Blue smoke? (oil burning) / Black smoke? (rich running)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any timing or misfire codes?
* Cooling System — Check for coolant leaks; water pump pulley belt can fail
* MOT History — Check for emissions failures and corrosion
